proficiencyLevels.name: Absolute Beginner
proficiencyLevels.description: Start from zero — learn the alphabet, basic greetings, and survival phrases in English

sections.title: Everyday Basics
sections.description: Learn essential words and phrases for daily life in English

units.title: Greetings & Introductions
objectives: Say hello and goodbye in formal and informal settings
units.grammarFocus: Basic sentence structure: Subject + Verb (I am / My name is...)
units.culturalNote: In English, 'Hello' and 'Hi' are both common. 'Hi' is more casual. 'Good morning' is used before noon.

exercises.instruction: Listen and read the sentence. Tap each word to see its meaning.
Complete the conversation by choosing the correct response.
Match the words with their meanings.
Arrange the words to form a correct sentence.
Fill in the blank with the correct word.
exercises.hint: Think about how to say your name in English.

content.rule: We use 'My name is' to introduce ourselves. 'Name' is a noun.
content.question: _____ name is Thomas. (Introducing yourself)
content.explanation: 'My' shows possession. We say 'My name is...' not 'I name is...'.

exercises.instruction: Read the text and write what it means in your own words.

content.prompt: Read the following and explain in simple English:
content.text: Hello! My name is Maria. Good morning!
content.sampleAnswer: This is a greeting. The person says hello, introduces herself as Maria, and says good morning.

units.title: Numbers 1-20
units.grammarFocus: Number words and how to use them in sentences (I have two...)
units.culturalNote: In English we count on our fingers. Numbers are used everywhere: ages, prices, phone numbers.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: How many siblings do you have?
dialogue.translation: How many siblings do you have?

exercises.instruction: Match the number words with their meanings.
Arrange the words to form a correct sentence.
Fill in the blank with the correct number word.
exercises.hint: The number in parentheses is your clue.

content.rule: With 'I' we use 'have'. With 'he/she' we use 'has'.
content.question: I _____ three cats. (I have three cats.)
content.explanation: 'Have' is used with 'I', 'you', 'we', 'they'. 'Has' is used with 'he', 'she', 'it'.

exercises.instruction: Read the text and write what it means.

content.prompt: Read the following and write what it means:
content.text: I have five books and three pens.
content.sampleAnswer: I have five books and three pens.

units.title: Colors & Descriptions
units.grammarFocus: Adjective placement: The car is red. / the red car

content.translation: Describing the color of a car.

exercises.instruction: Match the colors with their descriptions.
Arrange the words to form a correct sentence.
Fill in the blank with the correct color.

content.rule: After 'is' we use the adjective alone: The house is white.
content.question: The house is _____. (The house is white.)
content.explanation: In English, adjectives don't change form. We say 'The house is white' not 'The house is whites'.

exercises.instruction: Read and describe in your own words.

content.text: The cat is black and white.
content.sampleAnswer: The cat is black and white.

units.title: The Alphabet & Pronunciation

newVocabulary.definition: to say letters one by one

units.grammarFocus: English alphabet — 26 letters, vowels (A, E, I, O, U) and consonants
units.culturalNote: When spelling on the phone, English speakers often use words: A as in Apple, B as in Boy.

exercises.instruction: Listen to the English alphabet sounds.

content.sentence: A, B, C, D, E, F, G
content.translation: The first seven letters of the alphabet.

exercises.instruction: Match the letters with words that start with them.

content.prompt: The sound at the start of 'hello'

content.question: How many vowels are in the English alphabet?

content.rule: We use 'spell' to say letters one by one. 'How do you spell...?'
content.question: How do you _____ your name? (say the letters)
content.explanation: After 'do you' we use the base form of the verb: spell.

content.prompt: Write the word 'hello' letter by letter with hyphens:

units.title: Basic Phrases & Politeness
objectives: Use common polite phrases (please, thank you, excuse me)

newVocabulary.definition: polite way to get attention or apologize

units.grammarFocus: Short answers: Yes, I am. / No, I'm not. — contractions (I'm = I am)

content.translation: Polite way to get attention and ask about well-being.

options.text: Polite way to get attention or apologize

content.rule: I'm = I am. We use 'I'm' in casual speech.
content.question: _____ fine, thank you. (I am fine)
content.explanation: 'I'm' is the contraction of 'I am'. We use it in informal English.

content.prompt: Translate or explain in simple English:
content.text: Excuse me! How are you? I'm fine, thank you.
content.sampleAnswer: Excuse me! How are you? I'm fine, thank you.

units.title: Food & Drink Basics
objectives: Name common food and drink items
Say what you like and want
units.grammarFocus: I would like... / I want... — polite requests
units.culturalNote: In English we say 'a cup of coffee' or 'a glass of water'. We use 'some' with uncountable nouns.

content.translation: A polite request for coffee with milk.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: What would you like to drink?
dialogue.translation: What would you like to drink?

content.rule: 'Would like' is polite. 'Want' is more direct. We say 'I would like' or 'I'd like'.
content.question: I _____ like some tea, please.
content.explanation: 'Would like' is the polite way to request something. We say 'I would like' not 'I want' in formal situations.

content.text: I would like coffee and bread, please.
content.sampleAnswer: I would like coffee and bread, please. This is a polite request for food and drink.

units.title: Family Members
units.grammarFocus: Possessive: my mother, your father, his sister, her brother

content.rule: 'My' = belonging to me. 'Your' = belonging to you. We use them before nouns.
content.question: _____ sister is nice. (My sister is nice.)
content.explanation: 'My' shows that the sister belongs to the speaker. We say 'my sister' not 'I sister'.

content.text: My family is big. I have two brothers and one sister.
content.sampleAnswer: My family is big. I have two brothers and one sister.

units.title: Animals

newVocabulary.definition: an animal with feathers that flies
an animal that lives in water

units.grammarFocus: Articles: 'a' before consonants (a dog), 'an' before vowels (an animal)

content.sentence: I have a dog and a cat.

content.rule: Use 'a' before consonant sounds, 'an' before vowel sounds. 'Dog' starts with a consonant.
content.question: I have _____ dog. (I have a dog.)
content.explanation: We say 'a dog' because 'dog' starts with a consonant sound. We say 'an animal' because 'animal' starts with a vowel.

content.text: I have two dogs and three fish.
content.sampleAnswer: I have two dogs and three fish.

units.title: Body Parts

newVocabulary.definition: the top part of the body
the part at the end of the arm
the opening for eating and speaking

units.grammarFocus: My head hurts. — subject + verb + body part

options.text: the part at end of arm

content.rule: We say 'My head hurts' — possessive + body part + verb. 'Hurts' goes with he/she/it.
content.question: _____ head hurts. (My head hurts.)
content.explanation: We use 'my' to show it's our head. 'My head' is the subject. 'Hurts' is the verb.

content.text: My head hurts and my hand hurts too.
content.sampleAnswer: My head hurts and my hand hurts too.

units.title: Review & Practice
objectives: Practice greetings, numbers, colors, and family
units.grammarFocus: Review: my/your, have/has, I would like, basic adjectives

content.sentence: Hello! My name is Maria. I have two cats.
content.translation: A greeting and introduction with pets.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: Nice to meet you! How are you?
dialogue.translation: Nice to meet you! How are you?

options.text: My name is Thomas. / I'm fine, thank you!

exercises.instruction: Match all the words you've learned.

options.text: My female parent drinks a hot beverage.

content.question: How do you say 'Excuse me' in English?

content.rule: Review: We use 'my' before nouns. 'The cat' needs 'is' for the verb.
content.question: The cat _____ black. (The cat is black.)
content.explanation: With 'the cat' (singular) we use 'is'. With 'the cats' (plural) we use 'are'.

exercises.instruction: Write a short introduction in English based on this text.

content.prompt: Read and write a similar introduction about yourself:
content.text: Hello! My name is Maria. I have a sister and a brother. My cat is black. I like to drink coffee. Goodbye!
content.sampleAnswer: Hello! My name is [your name]. I have a sister and a brother. My cat is black. I like to drink coffee. Goodbye!

exercises.instruction: Match all the key vocabulary from Section 1.

content.question: Complete: 'I _____ Anna and I have two cats.'

exercises.instruction: Write a short self-introduction in English.

content.prompt: Write 3-5 sentences about yourself. Include: your name, your family, a pet or favorite thing, and a greeting.
content.sampleAnswer: Hello! My name is Thomas. I have a brother and a sister. My dog is brown. I like to drink tea. Goodbye!
